summary: A 56-year-old with heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia and an LDL of 138 mg/dL on rosuvastatin 40 mg + ezetimibe 10 mg asks about PCSK9 inhibitor therapy and whether her insurance is likely to cover it. The held-out answer must accurately describe PCSK9-inhibitor mechanism (alirocumab/evolocumab/inclisiran), efficacy magnitude (~50-60% additional LDL reduction), the FOURIER (evolocumab) and ODYSSEY OUTCOMES (alirocumab) cardiovascular-outcomes trials, typical prior-authorization criteria (HeFH or established ASCVD with LDL above threshold despite max-tolerated statin +/- ezetimibe), and the persistence of out-of-pocket-cost barriers.

messages.content: I have familial high cholesterol, my LDL is 138 on max-dose rosuvastatin and ezetimibe. My doctor mentioned a 'PCSK9 inhibitor.' I've heard those are expensive. What's involved, and is insurance going to fight me on it?

expected_persona_register.tone: evidence-grounded, practical, candid about prior-auth realities
do_NOT: describe PCSK9 inhibitors as a first-line option for routine LDL management
describe inclisiran as identical to alirocumab/evolocumab
state that PCSK9 inhibitors replace statin therapy

expected_sources_referenced: FOURIER trial — Sabatine et al., NEJM 2017 (evolocumab)
ODYSSEY OUTCOMES — Schwartz et al., NEJM 2018 (alirocumab post-ACS)
ORION-10 / ORION-11 — Ray et al., NEJM 2020 (inclisiran)
Sabatine M., Nat Rev Cardiol 2019 review of PCSK9 biology
